meta.title: MCP Why and How: Supernova Part 1

transcript: Hi everyone. This is the MCP final final final final final V2 final this time, for real. Um, we've been talking all year about MCP. Uh, I want to talk about the first steps to getting it into production. But first let me tell you why we've been talking about it all year. Uh, Solana is fundamentally for trading and about trading, and it has been from day one. That's why we started off with Serum. But now we're here, we have these new prop AMMs. And the question is how do we accelerate that, make it better at the protocol layer. So let me talk you through some examples. Uh, usually in finance we have these kind of common value things that look like auctions. So here the yellow, we don't know where those candles are going because that hasn't happened yet. But the green and red, those are candles that have already happened. So that's our setting here. And usually we'll have something like a prop AMM oracle update that will happen at various times. And one thing that's interesting in our current structure is there's a single leader, and so everything goes through that leader. And so that leader gets to act slightly later because they can insert, they can co-locate with their validator and they can insert things right at the end. But what happens when that leader decides they want to be the only person who's allowed to submit new information to the chain? In the current protocol, they actually can do that. And we actually are seeing some interesting stuff on on mainnet where uh oracles are not getting in for various reasons because of late packing or because of other other reasons. And so uh this has kind of transformed from not necessarily a theoretical attack to something that's going on right now on mainnet with late packing. So we want to guarantee with MCP that censorship resistance is impossible. So, um, another thing that we're maybe interested in, and we're not going to talk about too much how to solve it today, but just so you understand where we're going, is the because the the leader gets to bid later, he gets to see a little bit more of the price data. And as an auction theorist, that makes the auction much less efficient. And the idea is that if the price goes up, like uh like here, the price went up, he'll get outbid by the guy who bids later. If the price goes down, he's actually overbid. This causes what we call unraveling in financial markets, and it makes the market much less efficient. So what happens, how do we fix that? Well if we could hide the oracle update from the leader, AND we had censorship resistance, you can do a mixed strategy if you're the player who acts earlier. And that mixed strategy will allow you to still play the game without that complete unraveling that we're worried about. And moreover, the less time advantage you get, the more we limit that difference between the leader and the oracle updater who's not the leader, the less distortion there is in this mixed strategy model. So that gives us two properties that we can formally define that we're trying to achieve. And and today I'll be talking about how to achieve selective censorship resistance. It's very simple. When you submit a transaction to the chain, either you should get in or nobody should get in. That's fairness. Another property that we might want, and it's related to the mixed strategy that I talked about, is hiding. And there's two definitions up here. One of them is valency, which is you can sort of think of like finality but before any node knows it's final. Just means the last slot such that no matter what happens from that point onwards, the things in the log are going to get confirmed. And that allows us to define a hiding property which could also be called pre-trade privacy. Basically says that an adversary can't guess what's in an honest leader's block before the protocol becomes valent. So let's talk about how to actually achieve that. Uh, stop talking about what and start talking about when and how. Um, here is a example of the modified flow that we would have going from Alpenglow to Supernova Part 1, which is the kind of minimal version of MCP that will get us to multiple leaders and censorship resistance. Every box on here, as Brandon said he had a similar graphic, is a validator. They're just chosen to participate in different roles. And as you can see in Alpenglow that's also the case. So we have clients sending transactions to a leader. That leader sends shreds to relays or Turbine routes. And those those get sent to validators who vote and participate in Alpenglow's voter consensus protocol. So the flow is actually quite similar in Supernova Part 1. The client sends transactions to proposers. Now there are multiple of them, as we hoped. We ended the leader monopoly. Those proposers each send shreds to the relays or Turbine routes. And then those relays immediately forward those shreds to the validators as they do today in the retransmit stage. Hopefully if you're running Agave you're using zero copy so that happens faster. Um, but another thing is happening. The relays are sending one more message, which is an attestation, to the leader. And the leader is aggregating those attestation messages and that's the block that we actually come to consensus on. And I'll talk a little bit in more detail, that'll be the most technical part of my talk, is how does that happen. So just as a recap of Turbine, we use this core technology called erasure coding which is super sick and it's one of the coolest parts of Solana. It lets us drop some packets and not be too worried about message delivery. And the structure kind of looks like this. You shred the block into erasure coded packets, you send it to the relays or Turbine routes, and they broadcast it on to everybody else. We're keeping that same structure here, only we have multiple proposers. They each create a block of transactions, shred those blocks into erasure coded packets, and send them to the Turbine routes or relays. Then those relays will immediately broadcast them on to the validators as they do today. But another thing that they'll do is they will send messages to the consensus leader about which shreds they've received. They will send signed IHAVE messages and we call those uh relay attestations. They're attesting to which shreds they've received. Now the consensus leader, oh let me let me just go a little bit into what that is. Basically it's just a mapping, just a dictionary between what proposer one uh sent in the root of their shred. So all it is is proposer one sent this block, that's just the digest of the block or the block ID. So once you have sent all of those to the consensus leader, it's the consensus leader's job to pack an aggregate block which has an aggregation of the attestations. That's what actually gets sent out. What does that look like? Well it's just a matrix aggregating those attestations in the structure that you see here. So once they send that out, validator's job is to vote in Alpenglow, in voter. But we have a slightly different uh voter validation protocol here. So first of all, the aggregation must include an attestation from at least 60% of the relays. Second, if any block has 40% of shreds present in the aggregation that are valid, then that block is scheduled to be included. If it has less than 40% it won't be included. And then if we have 20% shreds, that means that we can reconstruct the block. And all validators reconstruct the block locally and make sure it reconstructs before voting. And they can do that without a delay because they should have received the shreds already. Now what do we do with all these proposals? Uh we take them, we order them by priority fee and dedup them, and then we execute them in order with some additional fee payer checks to prevent DoS. So that's basically the whole protocol. And what we tried to do with this is basically give the minimal thing that gets us to censorship resistance as fast as possible, but is also safe and live. And as I said we've been through so many of these designs. So on the top you can see Alpenglow here and uh it's great, it's uh you know provably bandwidth and latency optimal, but it doesn't have censorship resistance which is what we're trying to achieve. A couple years ago we proposed a protocol called Multiplicity that worked with Tendermint. The key issue there was that the proposer or the leader, the leader still had too much power to censor proposals so we didn't achieve that full uh censorship resistance property that we had. BRAID was a a proposal where each uh each thread was a separate consensus algorithm. The issue with that is the intricacy of consensus means that each consensus thread can sometimes hang and so you go at the pace of the slowest chain. That's that log K factor there which can sometimes be pretty bad. Uh then we had this other thing that I think maybe Toly suggested at some point which was what if we just send all the blocks on Turbine and then we vote on the aggregate of them. The issue with that is it multiplies the failure rate from Byzantine nodes by a factor of the number of proposers. In Chicago at Core Dev day I presented this protocol called GNR um and it was kind of almost what we see here. The issue was there was a kind of slight liveness issue and then there were some latency and bandwidth issues. We fixed them in Supernova Part 1 and so that's what we want to bring. As Brandon said we're collecting the receipts of everybody who says we're not going to be able to ship this thing quickly and I will be posting them on Twitter. So you have you have five minutes to delete. I'm going to go backstage and uh save the folder, okay? Thank you guys.

AI & Machine Learning
# Announcing Model Context Protocol (MCP) support for Google services
December 10, 2025
* []()
* []()
* []()
* []()
##### Michael Bachman
VP/GM, Google Cloud
##### Anna Berenberg
Engineering Fellow, Google Cloud
##### Try Gemini 3
Our most intelligent model is now available on Vertex AI and Gemini Enterprise
[Try now](https://console.cloud.google.com/vertex-ai/studio/multimodal)
With the recent launch of[Gemini 3](https://deepmind.google/models/gemini/), we have the state-of-the-art reasoning to help you learn, build, and plan anything. But for AI to truly be an “agent”, to pursue goals  and solve real-world problems on behalf of users, it needs more than just intelligence; it needs to reliably work with tools and data.
Anthropic’s[Model Context Protocol](https://www.anthropic.com/news/model-context-protocol)(MCP), often likened to a "USB-C for AI", has quickly become a common standard to connect AI models with data and tools. MCP enables AI applications to execute the complex multi-step tasks it takes to solve real world problems. However, implementing Google’s existing community-built servers often requires developers to identify, install, and manage individual, local MCP servers or deploy open-source solutions–placing the burden on developers, and often leading to fragile implementations.
Today we’re announcing the release of fully-managed, remote MCP servers. Google’s existing API infrastructure is now enhanced to support MCP, providing a unified layer across all Google and Google Cloud services. Developers can now simply point their AI agents or standard MCP clients like Gemini CLI to a globally-consistent and enterprise-ready endpoint for Google and Google Cloud services.
Crucially, we are extending this capability to your broader enterprise stack through Apigee, allowing you to leverage the purpose-built APIs your organization uses for specific data flows and business logic. Customers can now expose and govern their own developer-built APIs, as well as external third-party APIs, as discoverable tools for agents. Read more about Apigee’s announcement[here](https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/ai-machine-learning/mcp-support-for-apigee).
We are incrementally releasing MCP support for all our services, starting with:
1.**Google Maps**: Grounding AI in the real world
Maps Grounding Lite,[available through Google Maps Platform](https://developers.google.com/maps/ai/grounding-lite), connects AI agents to trusted geospatial data, offering access to fresh information on places, weather forecasts, and routing details such as distance and travel time. This allows developers to build agents that can accurately answer real-world location and travel queries without hallucinating. For example, an AI assistant can use Grounding Lite to respond to queries such as, "How far is the nearest park from this rental?”, “What should I pack for the weather in Los Angeles this weekend?”, or “Could you recommend kid-friendly restaurants near our hotel?”
2.**BigQuery**: Reasoning over enterprise data
The[BigQuery MCP server](https://docs.cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/use-bigquery-mcp)enables agents to natively interpret schemas and execute queries against enterprise data without the security risks or latency of moving data into context windows. It provides direct access to BigQuery features like forecasting while ensuring data remains in-place and governed.
3.**Google Compute Engine (GCE)**: Autonomous infrastructure management
By exposing

# AI companies want a new internet —and they think they’ve found the key
MCP has already taken the industry by storm, and now Anthropic is giving it away.
byHayden Field
Close
![Hayden Field](https://platform.theverge.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/2025/06/HAYDEN_BLURPLE.jpg?quality=90&strip=all&crop=0%2C0%2C100%2C100&w=2400)
## Hayden Field
Senior AI Reporter
Posts from this author will be added to your daily email digest and your homepage feed.
FollowFollow
[See All byHayden Field](https://www.theverge.com/authors/hayden-field)
Dec 10, 2025, 2:33 PM UTC
* Link
* Share
Image:> Cath Virginia / The Verge
[![Hayden Field](https://platform.theverge.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/2025/06/HAYDEN_BLURPLE.jpg?quality=90&strip=all&crop=0%2C0%2C100%2C100&w=96)](https://www.theverge.com/authors/hayden-field)
Hayden Field
Close
![Hayden Field](https://platform.theverge.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/2025/06/HAYDEN_BLURPLE.jpg?quality=90&strip=all&crop=0%2C0%2C100%2C100&w=2400)
## Hayden Field
Senior AI Reporter
Posts from this author will be added to your daily email digest and your homepage feed.
FollowFollow
[See All byHayden Field](https://www.theverge.com/authors/hayden-field)
is The Verge’s senior AI reporter. An AI beat reporter for more than five years, her work has also appeared in CNBC, MIT Technology Review, Wired UK, and other outlets.
* Link
* Share
Over the past 18 months, the largest AI companies in the world have quietly settled on an approach to building the next generation of apps and services —an approach that would allow AI agents from any company to easily access information and tools across the internet in a standardized way. It’s a key step toward building a usable ecosystem of AI agents that might actually pay off some of the enormous investments these companies have made, and it all starts with three letters: MCP.
MCP, or Model Context Protocol, began as a passion project from two Anthropic employees, but since its creation in mid-2024, it’s been widely adopted by companies like OpenAI, Google, Microsoft, and Cursor. There are even hints that Apple will use MCP in its forthcoming AI-enabled

sources.title: Microsoft Sentinel MCP server - Generally Available With Exciting New Capabilities
sources.snippet:
Microsoft Sentinel MCP server - Generally Available With Exciting New Capabilities | Microsoft Community Hub
## Blog Post
![](https://techcommunity.microsoft.com/t5/s/gxcuf89792/images/bS00NDcwMTI1LTFraU9sWQ?revision=11&image-dimensions=2000x2000&constrain-image=true)
Microsoft Security Community Blog
4 MIN READ
# Microsoft Sentinel MCP server - Generally Available With Exciting New Capabilities
[![OmriAmdursky's avatar](https://techcommunity.microsoft.com/t5/s/gxcuf89792/m_assets/avatars/default/avatar-11.svg?image-dimensions=50x50)](https://techcommunity.microsoft.com/users/omriamdursky/187549)
[OmriAmdursky](https://techcommunity.microsoft.com/users/omriamdursky/187549)
![Icon for Microsoft rank](https://techcommunity.microsoft.com/t5/s/gxcuf89792/images/cmstNC05WEo0blc?image-dimensions=100x16&constrain-image=true)Microsoft
Nov 18, 2025
Today, we’re excited to announce the General Availability of Microsoft Sentinel MCP (Model Context Protocol) server, a fully managed cloud service built on an open standard that empowers AI agents to seamlessly access your entire security context through natural language, eliminating the need for complex data engineering as you build agents. This unlocks new levels of AI agent performance and effectiveness, enabling them to do more for you.
Since the public preview launch on September 30, hundreds of customers have explored MCP tools that provide semantic access to their entire security context. These tools allow security AI agents to operate with unprecedented precision by understanding your unique security context in natural language.
Today, we’re introducing multiple innovations and new capabilities designed to help even more customers unlock more with AI-driven security.
This post offers a high-level overview of what’s new. Stay tuned for deep-dive blogs that will unpack each feature in detail.
### **Connect to Sentinel MCP server from Multiple AI Platforms**
By adopting the MCP open standard, we can progress on our mission to empower effective AI agents wherever you choose to run them. Beyond Security Copilot and VSCode Github Copilot, Sentinel MCP server is now natively integrated with**Copilot Studio**and**Microsoft****Foundry**agent-building experiences. When creating an agent in any of these platforms, you can easily select Sentinel MCP tools, no pre-configuration required. It’s ready to use, so if you are using any of these platforms, dive in and give it a try.[Click here for detailed guidance](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/sentinel/datalake/sentinel-mcp-get-started?branch=pr-en-us-307982#add-microsoft-sentinels-collection-of-mcp-tools)
![](https://techcommunity.microsoft.com/t5/s/gxcuf89792/images/bS00NDcwMTI1LWtDWVkwaA?image-dimensions=801x401&revision=11)Figure 1: Microsoft Copilot Studio
![](https://techcommunity.microsoft.com/t5/s/gxcuf89792/images/bS00NDcwMTI1LWdkc242Mw?image-dimensions=801x401&revision=11)Figure 2: Microsoft Foundry
Additionally, you can now connect**OpenAI ChatGPT**to Sentinel MCP server through a secured OAuth authentication through a simple configuration in Entra. [Learn how here](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/sentinel/datalake/sentinel-mcp-chatgpt-connector)
![](https://techcommunity.microsoft.com/t5/s/gxcuf89792/images/bS00NDcwMTI1LXYxc29jRA?image-dimensions=803x432&revision=11)Figure 3: ChatGPT uses Sentinel MCP tools to assess threat impact on your organization### **Custom KQL Tools**
Many organizations rely on a curated library of KQL queries for incident triage, investigation, and threat hunting used in manual Standard Operating Procedures (SOP) or SOAR playbooks—often managed within Defender Advanced Hunting. Now, with Sentinel MCP server, you can instantly transform these saved KQL queries into custom tools with just a click. This new capability allows you to empower your AI agents with precise, actionable data tailored to your unique security workflows. Once a KQL query is saved as a tool, Sentinel MCP

tags.impact_reason: MCP aims to address critical infrastructure issues like censorship resistance and MEV, which could improve Solana's performance, although its impact is still uncertain.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose a crystalline prism as the Semantic Anchor because it visually represents the core concept of the story: a single point of entry (the 'single leader monopoly') being transformed into multiple, parallel outputs ('Multiple Concurrent Proposers'). The fracturing of a single beam of light into a spectrum perfectly illustrates the protocol's function of breaking a bottleneck. The integrated text 'MCP' is diegetically etched into the prism itself, identifying the technology responsible for this fundamental architectural shift.
visual_idea.description: A massive, perfectly clear crystalline prism hangs suspended in a vast, dark architectural space. A single, powerful beam of white light enters the top of the prism, representing the old single-leader system. As the light passes through an internal facet, the letters 'MCP' are illuminated, subtly etched into the crystal. The beam then refracts and explodes out the bottom as a wide, orderly array of parallel, colored light beams, symbolizing the new Multiple Concurrent Proposers and the end of the bottleneck.

A single, powerful beam of white light enters the top of the prism, representing the old single-leader system. The beam then refracts and explodes out the bottom as a wide, orderly array of parallel, colored light beams, symbolizing the new Multiple Concurrent Proposers and the end of the bottleneck. The vast, dark architectural space features polished obsidian surfaces, creating deep, reflective textures that absorb ambient light, providing a stark contrast to the brilliant prism. Caustic lighting effects are highly detailed, showcasing intricate patterns of refracted light across the crystal and the obsidian surfaces. The composition emphasizes epic height and monumental scale, with significant negative space above the prism. Vertical aspect ratio (3:4). editorial.headline: Solana Unveils Supernova Protocol to End Leader Monopoly
editorial.standfirst: The network is shifting to Multiple Concurrent Proposers (MCP) to eliminate 'late packing' arbitrage and censorship. This architectural overhaul fundamentally alters the MEV landscape by enforcing pre-trade privacy and breaking the single-leader bottleneck.
key_takeaways: Deployment of **Supernova Part 1**, a specific implementation of MCP designed to decentralize block production.
New consensus logic requires **60% relay attestations** for aggregation and a **40% valid shred threshold** for block inclusion.
Elimination of **late packing** strategies, where single leaders exploit their position to insert transactions milliseconds before block finality.
editorial.article_body_markdown:
Solana is fundamentally restructuring its consensus layer to address the economic inefficiencies caused by single-leader block production. Under the current **Alpenglow** model, a single leader retains exclusive rights to submit information to the chain until the last possible moment. This monopoly has birthed a phenomenon known as **late packing**, where leaders co-locate with validators to insert transactions after other market participants, effectively censoring oracles and causing liquidity "unraveling" in financial markets. The solution, **Supernova Part 1**, operationalizes **Multiple Concurrent Proposers (MCP)** to enforce selective censorship resistance.

The new architecture bifurcates the role of block production. Instead of a single entity, multiple **proposers** simultaneously construct blocks and transmit erasure-coded shreds via **Turbine** routes to relays. These relays then send signed "attestations" (IHAVE messages) to a designated **Consensus Leader**. This leader's role is reduced to aggregating these attestations into a matrix rather than dictating the transaction payload. For a block to be scheduled, the aggregation must include attestations from at least **60% of relays**, and the block itself must possess at least **40% valid shreds**. This ensures that no single entity can unilaterally exclude valid transactions or hoard the final slot for arbitrage.

For institutional investors and DeFi protocols, this shift is critical for market efficiency. By obscuring the oracle update from the leader and enforcing fairness, the protocol allows for mixed-strategy plays that prevent the degradation of liquidity. The design explicitly targets **pre-trade privacy**, ensuring that an adversary cannot predict the contents of an honest leader's block before the protocol reaches valency. This move moves Solana beyond the theoretical constraints of previous proposals like **Multiplicity** and **BRAID**, prioritizing immediate censorship resistance without sacrificing the network's signature speed.
